item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats! +300!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You think he's content? (neutralised)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one didn't really resonate with me too much. I like the concept, but it didn't really go anywhere for me. I can see how other people may have liked it, so I'll leave with a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://imgur.com/a/iRs7q obligitory reminder of SMT :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And… what's wrong with that? I don't quite get your reasoning. SCP-1518 would be, by your logic, just a real fountain that evaporates things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good old Scranton, PA. Wait, I know that area!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding MrWrong. This is essentially just a real practice with an additional "but he's alive".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will downvote this, upon re-reading. After my venture in Japanese Studies, this SCP is almost essentially the real life practice of self-mummification (with the added tidbit that it is a living Buddhist mummy). It does little to the concept it is based on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
concise and horrifying. +1
Maybe I'm more horrified by this SCP as I am Eastern Asian.
Attractive fusion of oriental tradition and SCP world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a real thing. its done today- its not a bad thing and its not looked at with horror by those who attend the monks to their deaths. its not done often nor lightly but its still a thing thats real. it looks like tibetan formal wear which is the group of buddhist who popularized/mythologized this sort of death, but i believe this sort of thing would be carried from monastery to monastery- as an example to other monks. in tibet it would be a good way to avoid them putting your body outside after death… i dont like the break in clinical tone or that the letter, but thats already been said. anyway this is one of the things i like about this site- the meditation is done in real life and its creepy but this sort of SCP blurs the real- in this universe the sort of thing might happen- a poor mahayana buddhist stuck in the clear light meditation… what i do know is that its very hard for the desiccating monk to keep himself/herself in full lotus/mudra(posture) because the tendons (before death occurs) harden and the muscles atrophy so they lose the ability to move back into the positions to accomplish zazen and yet they spend months/years in prep work trying to die in this meditation… there is more here then the mummy 'leaving his time behind him' thing but the only reason i'm complaining is jealousy. its a very good idea iMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mummy is not dead. He simply has nothing else to say.

What is happening in his head I shall leave to the reader's imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Allow me to rephrase. He has apparently been allowed to die/achieve Nirvana.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are we 100% sure he's actually dead now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you not get it? Sorry if that sounds insulting. But the guy had managed to keep himself alive for centuries…and only allowed himself to die after his apology had been given to the (descendents) of the person he loved and hurt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. its all good and i like the idea, but i won't up or downvote simply because it doesn't leave me feeling 'wow thats cool' or 'what is this junk?' but rather 'ok…so?'
And another +100. Congrats, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this one, I think that it was simple and well executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A point that bugs me — and it's really minimal — is the bit about no audio recording equipment being available. Why wouldn't it be? Why would we go to the trouble of mentioning that it wasn't?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful. Sad and scary all at once. And how fitting that he should wind up with the Foundation, whose task is to maintain the weird in static perpetuity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gets an absolute upvote. The letter to the descendant was perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ultimate mediation for those trying to achieve nirvana

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After thinking over this some more, I still can't find any way to make 1520's dialogue more archaic without making it sound silly. I don't speak modern Japanese, let alone 16th century Japanese, and filling it with thees and thous and prithees just wouldn't work.

Since a lot of people have taken issue with it, i've decided to add a footnote explaining why its speech sounds "modern".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like what you've done here, though it lacks a bit of polish. A line here and there could be adjusted for tone, as it doesn't sound entirely clinical. The interview could either be adjusted to be more archaic sounding, as some have suggested, or alternatively you could replace it with Y's account of the exchange, accounting for any oddity in the wording as faulty recollection and paraphrasing. Overall, though, it doesn't need any major changes at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only the dehydrated-nearly-to-the-point-of-being-dead know peace from this rustling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really sure why I like this. Maybe because failed, flawed things always were a better scp material than something that works exactly as planned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh gosh, don't make me blush

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the tips. I was trying to have him say "Fear is an illusion", and mistakenly used Samsara to describe that. Upon further review, I think "Maya" is probably the term I was looking for.

To respond to your first comment, that's pretty much the idea I was going for. I see the monk as sort of a tragic figure - he wants to die so he can achieve what he set out to do, but he can't want to die because that would be desire, and thus a sign that he's not ready. And yet, at the same time, he's so old, and so far down the path, that he can't do anything else but wait for a death that never comes to him and to comtemplate mistakes and regrets that it's too late to do anything about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if the author had the intention when he/she wrote this SCP, but I found the fate of this 400 year old Buddhist monk very terrifying. Being "stuck" in the process of achieving nirvana (maybe forever) would be a living hell for this old monk. Upvote!

That being said, there's something a bit off in the interview I would like to discuss:

SCP-1520: You are afraid? Fear not. Fear is only an aspect of Samsara.

To be honest I'm really not sure about what "Fear is only an aspect of Samsara" means. Saṃsāra is a process that when an existence (sattva) dies, it's mind (not to be confused with soul) will be transferred and live as another existence (sattva). The reason to achieve nirvana is to break the cycle of Saṃsāra. I suggest this sentence be changed to "Death is only an aspect of Saṃsāra" instead.

SCP-1520: I have regrets. They pain my soul. I am unable to rid myself truly of desire while this work is undone. I must ask you to help me do what I cannot do for myself.

Like what I said above, a Buddhist monk use the concept of "soul" is just not right…. Buddhism do not use "soul" to describe what leaves the flesh when an existence (sattva) ceases to live. Maybe it will be better if you change it to "I have regrets, regrets that are holding me here."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He would probably refuse to drink any more than the bare minimum of water given to him. He's been waiting for his body to die for close to 400 years so he can attain enlightenment - he's conditioned himself to be free of desire, and probably considers it a sign of weakness that he even drinks the couple tablespoons the Foundation gives him to keep what's left of his blood from drying up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or if you gave him a Gatorade, would he suddenly 'POP' and go Sumo-sized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. Shinbokutsu is a good foundation to write a SCP on, and you created a nice twist by making it what is essentially a living mummy. The idea of someone living in a state like this is a bit horrifiying in itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Into MegaTen AND Homestuck? You may be my soulmate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so happy someone else thought of this immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it can be assumed that the writer responsible for translating the monk's conversation into English made the stylistic choice to modernize it for ease of understanding. I don't speak Japanese myself so I don't know how well any particular idioms would translate, and to my ears it would just sound silly if I tried to make it all flowery and Elizabethan. If anyone who does speak Japanese knows how I might improve it a bit, i'm open to suggestions.

The feeding process, I believe, was probably adopted by the Foundation from the maintenance regimen the temple was performing on the monk before they acquired him. It's probable that the monk doesn't need the cracker and bit of water to survive, since whatever's keeping him alive at this point is obviously not related to his diet. I imagine that the priests had been giving him offerings of food and beer and so on, and that at some point he requested that they cut down on it to the bare minimum.

As for complying with the request, I could probably add a sentence or two to explain the logic, but my feeling is that they determined the monk is of no threat, is acting in good faith, and that delivering its letter to the descendant could be easily covered up and thus would not constitute a containment breach, and might induce the monk to be more amicable to conversation in the future. Obviously that hasn't worked out to date, but it was worth the try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Buddhism SCPs are the shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not funny twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dehydrated buddhist-just add water!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awww yeah.

Matador? Dude, try getting your hands on a Trumpeter. The Gamzee-text still freaks me out, but hoo boy, is it useful…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MegaTen nerds represent!

I still get Matador flashbacks now and again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, it's the bit with the descendant living in Scranton that gets me. It really drives home the gulf of time between this poor monk and the woman he regrets. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would understand appearing to comply, in an attempt to get more information. Actually delivering that letter is what I don't get. Granted, it is an exceptionally low risk, with a believable explanation and good deniability.

I was prepared to hate this, just for being humanoid. Thank you for defying expectations and doing well what is often done poorly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this, although I find it a little odd that the Foundation was so quick to comply with the wishes of an SCP object, without even explanation. I mean, given its history, you'd think that the Foundation would be terrified that something would happen. Besides, it has no real reason to, other than sentimentality: and the Foundation doesn't tolerate sentimentality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with the chorus talking about the interview log issue.

SCP-1520's flesh is dried and translucent and is stretched taut over its bones.

Also, this sentence rustles my jimmies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just love this, though I'll third the "language should be archaic". If he's speaking 16th Century Japanese, Dr. Y there is no doubt going to have a hard time understanding him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did I. It has nothing to do with Wondertainment, though - plain old dung brick fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read that first as 'Deyhdrated bullshit - just add water!' That's got Wondertainment all over it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed it, but I agree about making the monk's dialogue a bit less current. Maybe remove some articles and move stuff around a bit, but if he starts turning into Yoda, you've gone too far!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if you did slowly dehydrate him? Is he an immortal who has been torturing himself for centuries, or is it truly his regret keeping him chained to his body? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Daisoujou!

/spams Agidyne

I'm sorry, I'll go to the corner now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dehydrated buddhist-just add water!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I've read of the "living mummy" thing before, and I was creeped out. The interview section is a little over dramatic, and I have to ask-why would the monk's words translate so easily into casual English? Perhaps change it into archaic or broken English too account for the difference.

Anyhow, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my attempt at meeting a couple of Bright's challenges at once - it having taken me about 2 hours from inspiration to posting it, and it being based on something creepy I read about on Wikipedia.

I eagerly await judgment.
